coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots enhance the biological nitrogen fixation by up-regulation of cellular metabolism and electron transport
coredata.dc:creator: Ouyang, Bowei
Zhong, Qinmei
Ouyang, Peng
Yuan, Yue
Wu, Xian
Yang, Sheng-Tao
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are useful nanomaterials of excellent water solubility, biocompatibility and optical stability for various applications. In this study, we achieved the nitrogen fixation activity enhancement of autogenous azotobacters (Azotobacter vinelandii) by GQDs and revealed its mechanism. GQDs (1–10 mg/L) accelerated the logarithmic growth phase of A. vinelandii, making bacteria enter the plateau earlier. Biocompatible GQDs did not cause cell death and oxidative damage of A. vinelandii. The expression levels of metabolism and nitrogen fixation related genes were significantly up-regulated by GQDs, thereby enhancing the activity of nitrogenase. The nitrogen fixation activity of A. vinelandii reached 471.7 % of the control upon the exposure to GQDs. The intracellular polyhydroxyalkanoate vesicles were consumed to provide energy for nitrogen fixation, which involved with a chain reaction of carbohydrate metabolism, lipid metabolism, and energy metabolism pathways accompanied by significant increases in electron transfer rate. The synergistic effect of GQDs on A. vinelandii further facilitated the augmentation of soil nitrogen content in Qinghai-Tibet Plateau. Our results provided an effective approach to enhance the biological nitrogen fixation by GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen fixation activity
Nitrogenase
Gene regulation
Environmental bioeffects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bacterial Growth and nitrogen fixation activity
Changes in membrane permeability and ultrastructural
Stimulation of growth and nitrogen fixation of A. vinelandii by GQDs
Uptake and ultrastructure modification of the A. vinelandii cells by GQDs
Enhanced of metabolic and electron transport activity of the A. vinelandii cells by GQDs
Mechanisms of BNF stimulation by GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau Research in Southwest Minzu University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support from the Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province, grant number 2023JDRC0014, and the Project of Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau Research in Southwest Minzu University, grant number 2024CXTD07.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization data of GQDs. (A) HRTEM images; (B) AFM image; (C) Raman and IR spectrum; (D) C1s XPS spectrum.

Stimulation of growth and nitrogen fixation of A. vinelandii by GQDs. (A) Growth curves; (B) Nitrogen-fixation activities; (C) NucGreen (all cells) & EthD-III (dead cells) staining; (D) Survival rate; (E) Oxidative stress. * p < 0.05 comparing to the control group.

Uptake and ultrastructure changes of A. vinelandii by GQDs. (A) Membrane permeability; (B) Uptake of GQDs in A. vinelandii cells; (C) SEM and TEM of A. vinelandii. * p < 0.05 comparing to the control group.

Metabolism and electron transport effects of A. vinelandii by GQDs. (A) Carbohydrate metabolism; (B) Lipid metabolism; (C) Energy metabolism; (D) CV curves; (E) Tafel curves; (F) ETSA. * p < 0.05 comparing to the control group.

Analysis of differentially expressed genes of A. vinelandii after exposure to GQDs. (A) Control vs 0.5 mg/L group; (B) Control vs 1 mg/L group; (C) Control vs 5 mg/L group; (D) Control vs 10 mg/L group.

Gene expression of the nitrogen fixation enhancement by GQDs. (A) Carbohydrate metabolism related genes levels; (B) Lipid metabolism related genes levels; (C) Energy metabolism related genes levels; (E) GO annotations analysis of up-regulated expressed genes exposure to GQDs.
Schematic diagram of the enhanced nitrogen fixation process by GQDs.
Changes of the soil nitrogen contents supplemented with A. vinelandii cells in the presence/absence of GQDs. *p < 0.05 compared with the control group.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots enhance the biological nitrogen fixation by up-regulation of cellular metabolism and electron transport

Yang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Pollution Control Chemistry and Environmental Functional Materials for Qinghai-Tibet Plateau of the National Ethnic Affairs Commission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are useful nanomaterials of excellent water solubility, biocompatibility and optical stability for various applications. In this study, we achieved the nitrogen fixation activity enhancement of autogenous azotobacters (Azotobacter vinelandii) by GQDs and revealed its mechanism. GQDs (1–10 mg/L) accelerated the logarithmic growth phase of A. vinelandii, making bacteria enter the plateau earlier. Biocompatible GQDs did not cause cell death and oxidative damage of A. vinelandii. The expression levels of metabolism and nitrogen fixation related genes were significantly up-regulated by GQDs, thereby enhancing the activity of nitrogenase. The nitrogen fixation activity of A. vinelandii reached 471.7 % of the control upon the exposure to GQDs. The intracellular polyhydroxyalkanoate vesicles were consumed to provide energy for nitrogen fixation, which involved with a chain reaction of carbohydrate metabolism, lipid metabolism, and energy metabolism pathways accompanied by significant increases in electron transfer rate. The synergistic effect of GQDs on A. vinelandii further facilitated the augmentation of soil nitrogen content in Qinghai-Tibet Plateau. Our results provided an effective approach to enhance the biological nitrogen fixation by GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen is the basic building element of creatures and the main nutrient that limits life on earth [1]. As a limiting nutrient, nitrogen affects the trophic biology and ecology of organisms worldwide, and ultimately their ecological distribution and abundance [2,3]. Nitrogen fixation is an important energy-intensive process of the environmental nitrogen cycle [4]. There are three main forms of nitrogen fixation, namely artificial nitrogen fixation (Haber-Bosch reaction), natural nitrogen fixation (lightning conversion) and biological nitrogen fixation (BNF, nitrogen-fixing microbial reduction) [5–7]. The economic and environmental costs of the extensive use of chemical nitrogen fertilizers in agricultural production are of global concern. The high nitrogen input and low efficiency of nitrogen use indicated that considerable amounts of nitrogen are lost to the environment [8], in forms of nitric oxide (NO) [9], nitrous oxide (N2O) [10], ammonia (NH3) emissions [11], and nitrate (NO3−) leaching [12]. This causes serious environmental problems such as soil acidification, air pollution and eutrophication [13]. BNF is the dominating form of nitrogen fixation, much higher than artificial nitrogen fixation and natural nitrogen fixation. BNF converts atmospheric nitrogen to ammonia through symbiotic, related, and free-living nitrogen-fixing microorganisms, and provides a substantial fixed nitrogen input to the soil [14]. These nitrogen-fixing microbes are found in a variety of habitats, including soil and marine environments, and live symbiotically with plants, particularly legumes [15,16]. Therefore, BNF is cheap and environmentally friendly, making it ideal for sustainable agriculture.

In the last two decades, great progress has been made in almost all aspects of BNF, and various technologies are developed to meet the demand for nitrogen fertilizers. For example, endosymbiotic nitrogen fixation by rhizomes and legumes [17–19], mutation and genetic engineering techniques can enhance BNF for crop production and agroforestry systems [17,20,21]. Of particular interest and importance is that nanomaterials might enhance nitrogen fixation in living organisms [22]. CdS nanoparticles (NPs) significantly enhanced BNF in pure microbial cultures [23]. Foliar application with iron oxide NPs stimulated nitrogen fixation, yield, and nutritional quality of soybean [24]. Similarly, carbon and Fe-Mo quantum dots (QDs) enhanced the activity of nitrogenase [25,26]. Antioxidant cobalt ferrite nanozyme as a bridge between nanotechnology and biological nitrogen fixation, which was shown to efficiently regulate the reactive oxygen metabolism and protect nitrogenase [27]. For future applications, more efficient nanomaterials are highly demanded and the underneath mechanisms of BNF enhancements should be revealed.

Compared to conventional metallic quantum dots, GQDs exhibited excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, and optical stability [28–30]. GQDs have extensive applications across diverse domains, particularly in biomedicine, cellular imaging, drug delivery, energy systems, and optoelectronic technologies owing to its exceptional performance. For instance, GQDs possess the capability to selectively induce DNA damage in cancer cells, thereby impeding tumor growth [29]. GQDs are suitable for tumor-specific imaging and drug delivery [31,32]. GQDs have also been used in the development of ultra-sensitive electrochemical sensors [33,34]. Similarly, GQDs can be used in the development of batteries, supercapacitors, high-performance light-emitting diodes and quantum transmission [35–37]. However, applications of GQDs to biological nitrogen fixation have not yet been reported. Previously, we found that reduced graphene oxide (RGO) stimulated the growth and nitrogen fixation activity of nitrogen-fixing bacteria [38]. Efficient contact with the sp2 carbon area without physical damage was proposed as the key factor of the BNF enhancements by RGO [38]. Due to the high solubility and small sizes, the sp2 carbon areas of GQDs would contact nitrogen-fixing bacteria gently, thus might benefit BNF. These results inspired us to investigate the effect of GQDs on the nitrogen fixing activity of A. vinelandii.

In this study, we investigated the impact of GQDs on A. vinelandii growth through comprehensive analysis including growth curves, fluorescence staining, electron microscopy, and assessment of oxidative stress parameters. The change of nitrogen fixing activity of A. vinelandii was measured by isotope ratio dilution method. Comprehensively, the nitrogen fixing gene expression and metabolic pathways were measured to reveal the mechanism of GQDs on nitrogen fixing activity by sequencing the bacterial genome. The concentrations of total nitrogen, ammonium nitrogen, and nitrate nitrogen were also quantified. The implications to the applications of GQDs in enhancing BNF and alleviating nitrogen loss are discussed.

GQDs were obtained from Suzhou Carbon Fung Graphene Technology, Ltd., China. GQDs were characterized by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan), AFM (Dimension FastScan, Bruker, USA), Raman (inVia, Renishaw Co., UK), IR (Magna-IR 750, Nicolet, USA), XPS (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo-Fisher, USA), UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Mapada, Shanghai, China) and FL (Dual-FL, Horiba, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial Growth and nitrogen fixation activity
ce:section.ce:para: A. vinelandii (BNCC337393) were purchased from BeNa culture collection and cultured following the recommended protocols. The cultivation protocols of A. vinelandii was provided in Supporting Information. For growth curve, the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was recorded at time intervals of 6 h for each flask. The growth curves were illustrated by plotting the OD values obtained within the time frame from 0 to 72 h. For survival rate, the bacterium suspensions (500 μL for each) were taken at 72 h, centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 10 min to remove the supernatant, and re-suspended in 200 μL of 0.85 % NaCl. To the cell suspension, 5 μL mixture of NucGreen stock solution and ethidium homodimer-III (EthD-III) stock solution (v:v of 1:2) were added. After thorough mixing by a vortex mixer, the mixture was incubated in dark for 15 min at room temperature. The fluorescence images were recorded on a fluorescence microscope (Axio Vert. A1, Zeiss, Germany) for cell counting. All bacteria were stained in green by NucGreen and only the dead ones were stained in red by EthD-III. The survival rates were calculated accordingly. For oxidative stress, the bacterial solution was collected and subjected to centrifugation at 5000 r/min for 5 min after a 72-hour incubation period. Then supernatant was carefully removed, and the cell pellet was resuspended in 1.0 mL of sterile PBS. The levels of total protein, total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC), superoxide dismutase (SOD), reduced glutathione (GSH) and malondialdehyde (MDA) were analyzed following the manufacturer’s instructions (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, Nanjing, China) on an UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Mapada, Shanghai, China).

The relative activity of nitrogenase was measured by the 15N-isotope tracer assay [39]. In a nutshell, KNO3 labeled with 15N (Adamas Co., Shanghai, China) was added to the 50 mL medium. Then, the medium of the aforementioned culture system was collected after 72 h incubation. After well shaking, 2 mL of the medium was collected and freeze-dried for 24 h. Each 2.0 mg of the freeze-dried sample was placed on a tin foil and embed. The samples were subjected into the IRMS (isoprime visION, Elementar, Germany) to determine the 15N:14N ratio after exhausting the air. The relative activity of nitrogenase was calculated accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in membrane permeability and ultrastructural
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of graphene on cell membrane permeability, we employed the crystal violet method, which relies on quantifying the concentration of crystal violet absorbed by cells to determine membrane permeability [40]. For intracellular GQDs determination, bacteria were collected by centrifugation. After collection by centrifugation and washing three times with 0.9 % saline, bacteria were homogenized at 60 Hz for 500 s, followed by Raman analysis (inVia, Renishaw Co., UK). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) techniques were employed to observe the ultrastructural alterations caused by GQDs. The cells were harvested after 72 h by centrifuging at a speed of 4000 r/min for a duration of 10 min. The cell residues were then suspended and treated with a solution containing 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for a period of 12 h to ensure fixation. Before SEM observation, the sample was treated with glutaraldehyde removal and washed three times with sterile distilled water. Then, the dehydration and drying method using methanol was adopted to dry the samples. Finally, the dried sample was firmly attached to the ion sputtering instrument (Mc100, Hitachi, Japan) and observed with an SEM (SU8100, Hitachi, Japan) 30 s after gold spraying. For TEM observation, the sample was embedded and ultra-thin sliced in the size range of 60–80 nm using a German-made ultramicrotome (LeicaUC7, Leica, Germany). Subsequently, the samples were photographed under TEM (HT7700, Hitachi, Japan). The process of electron microscopy testing was conducted by specialized companies (Servicebio Co., Wuhan, China).

To elucidate alterations in bacterial metabolic activity, we employed the recommended protocol (Elabscience Biotechnology Co., Ltd) for analyzing carbohydrate (hexokinase activity and glucose-6-phosphate content), lipid (lipase activity and glycerol content), and energy (ATPase activity and ATP content) metabolism of A. vinelandii with the aim of delving deeper into their metabolic pathways and biosynthesis mechanisms. The electrochemical behavior of A. vinelandii following exposure to GQDs was further investigated through the utilization of an electrochemical workstation (DH7000C, Jiangsu Donghua Testing Technology Co., ltd.), where cyclic voltammetry (CV) and Tafel tests were conducted. The CV and Tafel analyses were conducted using a conventional three-electrode setup comprising of platinum sheet electrodes clamp as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrodes as the reference electrode, and platinum wire electrodes as the counter electrode. The CV experiments were conducted within a voltage range of −1V to 1 V, employing a scan rate of 0.5 V/s. The Tafel test condition has an initial potential of −0.25 V, a final potential of 0.25 V, and a scan rate of 3 mV/s. Furthermore, to evaluate the impact of GQDs on electron transport systems activity (ETSA) during A. vinelandii nitrogen fixation, we employed a previously established method to assess the ETSA [40].

After 72 h of GQDs, A. vinelandii precipitates were collected by centrifugation at each reference point. The samples were quickly frozen in liquid nitrogen and transferred to a refrigerator at −80 °C prior to measurement. The transcriptomes analyses were performed using an Illumina HiSeqTM at Majorbio Bio-Pharm Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The soil samples were collected from Hongyuan County (102°34′55″, 32°49′58″) in Sichuan Province, China, and the experiments also were conducted in Hongyuan County. Soil water content was maintained at 60 % during whole experiments. The A. vinelandii with CFU at 109 and GQDs with concentration of 0, 1, 10 mg/kg added into soil with 50 g with three replicates. After 21 d incubation, 1 g soil was taken to estimate the total nitrogen content using the Kjeldahl method. To extract inorganic nitrogen, 10 g sieved soil was added into a wide-mouth bottle with KCl (final concentration 2 mol/L), shaken at 160 rpm for 1 h, and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatants were collected for the determination of ammonium and nitrate nitrogen using the MgO-Devarda distillation method [38,41].

All data were presented as the mean accompanied by the standard deviation (mean ± SD). Statistical significance was determined at p < 0.05 using Student's t-test. To identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs), gene expression analysis involved obtaining Read Counts of the genes and utilizing DESeq2, DEGseq, or edgeR. Functional annotation analysis of genes in the differential gene set was performed using Gene Ontology (GO), Clusters of Orthologous Groups (COG) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) databases. Significantly enhanced functions were identified when the P value (P adjustment) was less than 0.05. Data mining and Fig. presentation were conducted on Majorbio Cloud Platform's online platform at https://www.Majorbio.com.

The HRTEM images (Fig. 1 A) revealed the presence of GQDs in aggregated particles, exhibiting an average size of approximately 2 nm. Inset of Fig. 1A provided evidence of the exceptional crystallinity of GQDs, displaying a lattice fringe spacing measuring approximately 0.2251 nm, which corresponded to the crystal plane (100) of graphene. This finding aligns with the existing literature [42]. The AFM was used to analyze the thickness of GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1B. The height distribution of GQDs ranged from 0.5 to 2.8 nm [43]. Raman spectroscopy indicated the presence of a disorder-induced phonon mode in the GQDs sample, represented by the D band at 1415 cm−1 (Fig. 1C). Additionally, we observed the Raman-allowed E2g phonon mode vibrations through the G band at 1648 cm−1 [33,44]. The IR spectroscopy analysis revealed absorption peaks at 1588 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching of C=C bonds, 1398 cm−1 indicative of alkanes' C-H stretching rocking motion, and 1077 cm−1 associated with C-O-C [33,45]. The XPS measurement provided further evidence to support the findings of FTIR spectra. In the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1D), three peaks were attributed to C-C (284.2 eV), C-O (285.7 eV) and C=O (287.7 eV). The functionalization of hydroxyl and carbonyl on GQDs was indicated by the presence of C-C, C=O, and C-O bonds. The XPS spectrum of GQDs exhibited a similar pattern as reported in previous studies [46]. An evident absorption band at 266 nm was observed in the UV-vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs. Furthermore, the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of GQDs were observed to be 360 nm and 451 nm, respectively (refer to Fig. S1) [47]. The combined characterization data suggested that the GQDs samples exhibited favorable properties for conducting further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stimulation of growth and nitrogen fixation of A. vinelandii by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In our previous studies, low concentrations of graphene materials, including graphene oxide (GO) and RGO, stimulated the growth of A. vinelandii [38,41]. Therefore, the low concentrations of GQDs were selected for the experiments. The growth curve of A. vinelandii was recorded by measuring OD600 in the presence or absence of GQDs (Fig. 2 A). A. vinelandii growth rate showed a typical plateau after 60 h incubation in the control group. The growth rates were significantly stimulated by GQDs after 18 h incubation, and advanced entered the logarithmic growth phase, then reached a plateau in shorter time with GQDs concentrations increasing. These results were in line with previous studies that carbon dots at 2–20 mg/L did not inhibit the growth of A. chroococcum [26]. Copper NPs at 20 mg/L had little effect on the growth rate of A. vinelandii [48]. Even high concentrations of Fe-Mo QDs at 50–1000 mg/L had no significant inhibitory effect on A. chroococcum growth [25]. More importantly, 15N isotope dilution was used to determine nitrogen fixing enzyme activity [39]. The nitrogen fixing activity of A. vinelandii was significantly improved by 54.2 % with GQDs at 1 mg/L after 72 h incubation, equaling to 471.7 % of the control (Fig. 2B). Previously, the activity of A. chroococcum was increased by 158 % in treatment with carbon dots at 4 mg/L [26], and by 9.56 % in treatment with RGO at 500 mg/L compared to the control [38]. Obviously, our study evidenced the great promotion of BNF by GQDs with a much lower nanomaterial supplement.

The biocompatibility of GQDs was further confirmed through the assessment of A. vinelandii survival rate and oxidative stress response. The cells were stained with NucGreen stoste (live and dead cells) and EthD-III stoste (dead cells only) to quantify the survival rate of A. vinelandii cells (Fig. 2C). After an incubation for 72 h, there were only few dead cells in all GQDs groups (0.5–10 mg/L) and the control, and the corresponding survival rates were 96.6 %, 95.5 %, 96.2 %, 98.8 %, 96.1 % and 98.6 %, respectively (Fig. 2D). Moreover, oxidative stress is a recognized method in the safety assessment of graphene materials [49,50]. The oxidative stress parameters including T-AOC, SOD, GSH and MDA were relatively stable in the 0–10 mg/L range, and showed no dose-dependent pattern in this study (Fig. 2E). These results indicated that GQDs were well biocompatible with A. vinelandii. It was in line with previous studies that GQDs did not cause oxidative stress and reduced bacterial activity in antibacterial photodynamic action [51]. Beyond that, RGO and carbon nanotubes did not induce oxidative stress in bacteria, too [38,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and ultrastructure modification of the A. vinelandii cells by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The permeability of the membrane plays a pivotal role in facilitating the cellular uptake specificity of GQDs by A. vinelandii, and was investigated through crystal violet staining and Raman spectroscopy [40]. The results indicated that GQDs slightly reduced the permeability of the cellular membrane of A. vinelandii across all GQDs concentrations (Fig. 3 A). We speculated that the internalization of GQDs by cells occurs through passive mechanisms. A positive correlation between the concentration of intracellular GQDs and cellular uptake provided further evidence for passive cell absorption (Fig. 3B). Additionally, the GQDs modified the ultrastructure of A. vinelandii cells (Figs. 3C & S2). SEM observations showed complete ovoid A. vinelandii cells in the control group. At 1 mg/L of GQDs, the cell wall of A. vinelandii remained intact, and the cellular shape changed from oval to rod-like. At 10 mg/L, the changes in cell elongation were more obvious and the cell wall was slightly crumpled, which might be hyperextension, as we first found. Under TEM, the cells in the control group showed normal ultrastructure and were filled with numerous vesicles. These vesicles are called polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHA) that protect bacteria from oxygen, dry them out and store energy, acting like body fats [39]. However, the intracellular vesicles of A. vinelandii disappeared and the ultrastructure of A. vinelandii cells changed into rod-like, with clearly visible cell membrane and cytoplasm at GQDs of 1 mg/L and 10 mg/L. Vesicle formation is reversible in response to the availability of the carbon or nitrogen source [53]. Considering the nontoxic nature of GQDs (Fig. 2D & 2E), we speculated that the missed PHA vesicles should be exploited by cells through other pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced of metabolic and electron transport activity of the A. vinelandii cells by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that GQDs have a promoting effect on the metabolic rate of cells, including carbohydrate metabolism, lipid metabolism and energy metabolism. In the presence of GQDs (0.5–10 mg/L), the activity of hexokinase was significantly increased by 239.9 %, 233.1 %, 168.6 %, and 513.4 %, respectively (Fig. 4 A). The content of metabolite glucose-6-phosphate was also significantly increased, especially in the 0.5 mg/L group, reaching a value of 548.2 % compared to the control group. This implied that the initial step in carbohydrate metabolism involved the enzymatic action of hexokinase, which catalyzes the conversion of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate [54]. The stimulation of GQDs significantly enhanced the glucose metabolism activity of cells to supply the energy required for nitrogen fixation. Furthermore, lipase activity in the GQDs groups (0.5–5 mg/L) increased by 123.7 %, 167.0 %, and 120.2 % compared to the control group (Fig. 4B). Glycerol content showed a dependent decrease with decreasing GQDs concentration because it was not only involved in cell membrane synthesis but also in metabolism through the carbohydrate pathway [55]. The findings further supported the utilization of cellular PHA vesicles for nitrogen fixation. Additionally, the feasibility of cellular energy metabolism was investigated to explore the substantial energy demand for nitrogen fixation. The ATPase activity was significantly increased by 156.2 %, 149.7 % and 158.9 % in the 1–10 mg/L group, compared with the control group (Fig. 4C). ATP content also significantly increased by 136.4 %, 161.1 % and 200.3 % of control group. Therefore, GQDs exerted a significant stimulating effect on the carbon source metabolism of A. vinelandii to facilitate the process of biological nitrogen fixation.

In the process of biological nitrogen fixation, electron transport performance is considered the key factor in determining reduction efficiency [56]. CV, Tafel and ETSA analysis were conducted to investigate the electrochemical process and intracellular electron transport activity [40]. A significant redox peak appears in the CV curve after adding GQDs (1 mg/L). At 0.083/−0.118 V, the peak current of the supply system significantly increased to −0.153/0.816 mA compared to the control group peak current of −0.107/0.698 mA at 0.273/−0.152 V (Fig. 4D). Interestingly, the peak current (−0.247 mA) corresponding to the oxidation peak (0.055 V) in the 10 mg/L group exceeded that in the control group. The increase in the peak current indicated that the presence of more redox active substances enhanced electron transport efficiency. By extrapolating the Tafel curve, we obtain exchange current densities of 1.69 × 10-4 A/cm−2 (control), 2.18 × 10-4 A/cm−2 (1 mg/L), and 5.99 × 10-5 A/cm−2 (10 mg/L), respectively (Fig. 4E). As the exchange current density increases, the reaction becomes more facile to execute. This observation implies GQDs enhances the rate of electron transfer. Moreover, in the presence of GQDs (0.5–10 mg/L), ETSA exhibited significantly higher values compared to the control, with percentages of 379.7 %, 239.6 %, 283.3 %, and 237.2 %, respectively (Fig. 4F). Collectively, CV, Tafel, and ETSA analysis validated that the application of GQDs could expedite electron transfer efficiency to facilitate biological nitrogen fixation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of BNF stimulation by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional classification of the upregulated DEGs was analyzed by GO, COG and KEGG analyses. In the GO functional annotation, these functional groups were classified into “molecular functions” and “biological processes” (Fig. 6E). In the “molecular functions” category, most DEGs were labeled to participate in DNA binding, ATP binding, metal ion binding, catalytic activity, transferase activity, transmembrane transporter activity and electron transfer activity in all GQDs treatments (especially 1 mg/L), which were the main processes involved in nitrogen fixing function [60]. In the “biological processes” category, there were a large number of labeled genes involved in transmembrane transport, methylation, nitrogen fixation, glycolytic process, lipid metabolic process, and siderophore biosynthetic process, which were involved in cellular metabolism and provide energy for nitrogen fixation [60]. The COG functional annotation classifies these functional groups into three categories: “metabolism”, “information storage and processing”, and “cellular processes and signaling” (Fig. S3). The category of “metabolism” includes a wide range of genes involved in various cellular metabolic pathways, such as the metabolism of inorganic salts, carbohydrates, and amino acids. This finding further supports the effectiveness of GQDs in enhancing cellular metabolic activity. The up-regulated genes in the category of “information storage and processing” are responsible for transcribing and translating cellular nucleic acids to ensure the synthesis of functional proteins, particularly nitrogenase, downstream. Similarly, the up-regulated genes were classified under “cellular processes and signaling,” suggesting that GQDs have potential applications in improving cell signaling and facilitating biological nitrogen fixation. The impact of GQDs stimulation on the biological functions of A. vinelandii was further examined through KEGG functional annotation analysis (Fig. S4). The KEGG pathway analysis revealed that exposure to GQDs (0.5, 1, and 5 mg/L) induced significant transcriptional changes in A. vinelandii associated with numerous genes involved in carbohydrate metabolism, lipid metabolism, and energy metabolism upon treatment with GQDs (Fig. S5-8). These metabolic pathways are closely linked to PHA production.

Based on aforementioned results, a mechanism was proposed for the GQDs enhanced nitrogen fixation process of A. vinelandii (Fig. 7 ). GQDs stimulated the up-regulated DEGs, including metabolism and nitrogen fixation related genes in A. vinelandii cells. Mo-Fe, V-Fe and Fe-Fe nitrogenases thus had excessive reducing power to promote the fixation of atmospheric N2, which required large amount of energy. Intracellular PHA vesicles were broken down to supply energy for ATP synthesis, simultaneously, the carbohydrate metabolism, lipid metabolism and energy metabolism pathways were driven to absorb carbon source from glucose. Furthermore, GQDs can enhance the efficiency of electron transfer, thereby improving the overall effectiveness of biological nitrogen fixation. To sum up, an appropriate amount of GQDs (e.g., 1 mg/L) could promote the BNF of A. vinelandii through the regulation of related gene expression, which is a sustainable approach to improving soil fertility. The utilization of BNF not only yields economic benefits for producers, but also fosters a reduction in agriculture's reliance on synthetic fertilizers, thereby bestowing environmental advantages upon society as a whole.

Since plants cannot absorb nitrogen directly from the air, they need external fixed nitrogen to grow and develop. However, the manufacture and use of chemical nitrogen fertilizers, while increasing cash crop yields, also bring environmental pollution. Therefore, biological nitrogen fixation has great potential as an alternative to chemical nitrogen fertilizers. We performed an experiment in Qinghai-Tibet Plateau (Hongyuan county, Sichuan Province, China) by adding GQDs-treated A. vinelandii to local soil. The soil was subsequently inoculated with GQDs-treated A. vinelandii and incubated for a duration of 21 days. We observed a 6.2 % increase in soil total nitrogen upon the addition of A. vinelandii (Fig. 8 ). The addition of bacteria treated with GQDs at concentrations of 1 mg/L and 10 mg/L resulted in soil nitrogen content increases of 23.9 % and 15.6 %, respectively. Especially in the inorganic nitrogen available to plants, the addition of A. vinelandii significantly increased the ammonia nitrogen in soil (34.4 %). Similarly, A. vinelandii treated with GQDs also increased ammonia nitrogen and nitrate nitrogen in the soil. The majority of nitrogen increase should be attributed to the biological nitrogen, which was utilized by A. vinelandii for biomolecule synthesis [28]. The same findings have been observed in relation to other types of graphene materials [38,41]. The increase in total nitrogen content in the soil would hopefully alleviate the nitrogen loss, especially for Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, which loses large quantity of nitrogen each year.

In summary, we investigated the effects of GQDs on the nitrogen fixation activity of A. vinelandii and evaluated the expression of key genes involved in nitrogen fixation process. GQDs accelerated the logarithmic growth phase of A. vinelandii and were biocompatible. GQDs promoted cell metabolic rate and nitrogenase activities (Mo-Fe, V-Fe, and Fe- Fe nitrogenase) by stimulating the up-regulation of metabolism gene and nitrogen-fixing gene expression. Along with the BNF increases, the intracellular PHA vesicles were consumed to sustain the energy required for nitrogen fixation, accompanied by a significant enhancement of electron transfer rate. The promotion of soil nitrogen content was facilitated by the augmentation of A. vinelandii nitrogen fixation through GQDs. Our results collectively indicated that GQDs have great potential in promoting the efficiency of biological nitrogen fixation. It is hoped that our findings would benefit the alleviation of nitrogen loss in Qinghai-Tibet Plateau and also other areas.

Bowei Ouyang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qinmei Zhong: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Peng Ouyang: Visualization, Methodology. Yue Yuan: Project administration, Methodology. Xian Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology. Sheng-Tao Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province, grant number 2023JDRC0014, and the Project of Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau Research in Southwest Minzu University, grant number 2024CXTD07.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The Supporting Information is available free of charge on the website. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.150694.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
